does: This paper introduces PsychoBench, a comprehensive evaluation framework designed to measure psychological attributes of large language models by adapting thirteen psychological scales from clinical psychology. The framework organizes these scales into four categories—personality traits, interpersonal relationships, motivational tests, and emotional abilities—and applies them to five prominent models including GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and LLaMA variants. The study systematically assesses whether and how LLMs exhibit psychological characteristics measurable by standardized instruments, and examines differences in psychological profiles across models, including comparisons between safety-aligned and jailbroken versions to reveal whether alignment training masks underlying psychological tendencies.
connects: This work builds on growing research examining LLM behavior and capabilities beyond standard NLP benchmarks, extending prior investigations into emergent properties and alignment mechanisms in large models. It responds to increasing interest in understanding LLMs as interactive agents that occupy diverse societal roles, connecting to literature on AI interpretability and behavioral characterization. The paper relates to prior work on evaluating model safety and alignment, drawing on jailbreak techniques used to probe hidden model behaviors, and contributes to emerging discourse about anthropomorphization of AI systems and whether LLMs exhibit genuine psychological properties or merely simulate human-like responses through training patterns.
enables: This framework enables future investigations into whether psychological consistency or coherence emerges in LLMs and how different training procedures affect psychological profiles of models. It provides tools for monitoring psychological drift in increasingly powerful models and potentially detecting undesirable personality-like characteristics that alignment training may incompletely suppress. The work opens research directions on using psychological assessment as an additional evaluation modality for model safety and trustworthiness, and supports development of better alignment techniques by identifying psychological dimensions that escape current oversight. It also enables comparative psychology studies examining whether different architectural choices, scale, or training paradigms produce systematically different psychological manifestations in artificial agents.
